Removing limit-up-estimated candidates destroys much of the alpha, showing that A-share short-horizon opportunities concentrate around strong limit-up states.
Removing limit-down-estimated candidates has little impact, suggesting the strategy is not driven by naive limit-down reversal.
Broken-limit filtering has limited effect, supporting that the main signal is strong-state continuation rather than broken-board noise.
Liquidity filter exposes a capacity trade-off: stricter liquidity constraints reduce return and breadth.
